questions.question: Why is BenchmarkDotNet preferred over a hand-rolled `Stopwatch` loop for micro-benchmarking a hot method?
options: It runs warmup, many iterations, and statistical analysis in an isolated Release process, whereas a `Stopwatch` loop mostly measures JIT warmup and noise
`Stopwatch` cannot measure intervals shorter than one millisecond, so it is useless for fast methods
BenchmarkDotNet runs the benchmark in Debug configuration, which produces more stable numbers
BenchmarkDotNet disables the garbage collector during measurement so allocations never affect results
questions.explanation: BenchmarkDotNet isolates each benchmark in its own Release-config process, handles warmup and many iterations, and does statistical analysis, whereas a raw `Stopwatch` loop measures JIT warmup and noise. It runs in Release (not Debug) and does not disable the GC. `Stopwatch` resolution is sub-millisecond, so that distractor is also false.

questions.question: What does adding `[MemoryDiagnoser]` to a BenchmarkDotNet class report?
options: Bytes allocated and Gen0/1/2 collection counts per operation
The peak managed heap size reached during the whole run
The number of CPU cache misses per operation
The wall-clock time spent inside the garbage collector across all iterations
questions.explanation: `[MemoryDiagnoser]` reports allocated bytes plus Gen0/1/2 collection counts per operation, which is usually more actionable than nanoseconds. It does not measure cache misses, total GC wall-clock, or peak heap size.

questions.question: In a high-throughput service, reducing the number of Gen0 allocations per request generally reduces GC frequency and improves p99 tail latency.
questions.explanation: Fewer allocations fill Gen0 more slowly, triggering fewer collections and less pause/CPU time, which improves throughput and tail latency. Reducing allocations is the consumer-side lever you control, as opposed to tuning the collector itself.

questions.question: Which type should you use to hold a buffer that must remain valid across an `await` in an async method?
options: `Memory<T>` (or `ReadOnlyMemory<T>`)
`Span<T>`
`ReadOnlySpan<T>`
A `stackalloc`-backed `Span<T>`
questions.explanation: `Span<T>` is a `ref struct` restricted to the stack and cannot cross an `await`. `Memory<T>`/`ReadOnlyMemory<T>` can live on the heap and cross awaits; call `.Span` only inside synchronous regions.

questions.question: How many times may a `ValueTask`/`ValueTask<T>` returned by a method safely be awaited?
options: Exactly once
Any number of times, just like a `Task`
Twice, but only if the second await happens after completion
Zero times â€” you must call `.AsTask()` before awaiting it
questions.explanation: `ValueTask` is consume-once: await it exactly once, and never await twice, read `.Result` before completion, or store it. If you need multi-await semantics, call `.AsTask()` once and await the resulting `Task`.

questions.question: A developer claims `StringBuilder` is *always* faster than `+` for string concatenation. What is the correct senior nuance?
options: For just 2-3 concatenations, `+` (compiled to `String.Concat`) is faster and allocates less; `StringBuilder` wins on loops or unknown counts
`StringBuilder` is always faster because `string` is immutable and `+` re-allocates every time regardless of count
`+` is always faster because the compiler folds all concatenations into a single allocation at any size
They are identical because the C# compiler rewrites every `StringBuilder.Append` chain into `String.Concat`
questions.explanation: For a few concatenations `+` lowers to a single `String.Concat` call that is faster and lower-allocation; `StringBuilder` only wins on loops or unknown/large counts. The compiler does not fold loop concatenations or rewrite `StringBuilder` into `String.Concat`.

questions.question: Which of the following are common sources of *hidden* heap allocations on a hot path that a profiler would flag? (Select all that apply.)
options: Boxing a value type to `object` or an interface
Lambda closures that capture local variables
`string.Format` with integer arguments (boxes the ints and allocates an `object[]`)
Iterating a `ReadOnlySpan<int>` with a `foreach` loop
Reading a field of a `readonly struct` through a `readonly` reference
questions.explanation: Boxing, capturing closures, and `string.Format` (boxes ints + allocates a `params object[]`) all allocate. Iterating a span allocates nothing (no iterator/closure), and accessing a `readonly struct` through a `readonly` reference is exactly the case where the compiler can skip the defensive copy.

questions.question: Given the code below, after calling `Move()` once, what is the value of `_p.X`?
options: 0 â€” `_p` is a `readonly` field of a mutable struct, so `Shift()` runs on a discarded defensive copy
1 â€” `Shift()` mutates the field in place because `X` is public
It does not compile â€” calling a mutating method on a `readonly` field is a compile error
It throws at runtime because mutating a `readonly` struct field is not allowed
questions.explanation: Because `Point` is a mutable struct behind a `readonly` field, the compiler makes a defensive copy before invoking `Shift()`, so the mutation is discarded and `_p.X` stays 0. It compiles fine and throws nothing â€” making `Point` a `readonly struct` would surface the bug by making the mutating method illegal.

questions.question: When is passing a value type by `in` parameter the right call?
options: For a large `readonly struct`, to pass a readonly reference instead of copying all fields on every call
For any struct, since passing by reference is always cheaper than copying
For small structs like an `int`-sized value, where the indirection beats the copy
For mutable structs, so the callee can modify the caller's copy in place
questions.explanation: `in` shines for large readonly structs, avoiding a full field copy on the call. For small structs the copy is cheaper than the indirection, and `in` is readonly so the callee cannot mutate the caller's value.

questions.question: Which statements about `ArrayPool<T>.Shared` are correct? (Select all that apply.)
options: The rented array may be larger than the size you requested, so you must track and use only the length you asked for
You should return the array in a `finally` block and never read or write it after returning
A returned array may contain stale data unless you pass `clearArray: true` on return
`Return` zeroes the array by default, so secrets left in a rented buffer are always cleared automatically
After renting, the array is guaranteed to be exactly the requested length
questions.explanation: Rented arrays may be larger than requested, must be returned in a `finally` and never touched after return, and may hold stale data unless cleared with `clearArray: true`. `Return` does not clear by default, and the array is not guaranteed to be the exact requested length.

questions.question: On which kind of method does `ValueTask<T>` actually save an allocation compared to `Task<T>`?
options: A hot path that *often completes synchronously* (e.g. a cache hit), where `ValueTask` allocates nothing on the sync path
Any async method, because `ValueTask` is a struct and structs are never heap-allocated
A method that always completes asynchronously after real I/O, where `ValueTask` avoids the underlying `Task`
A method whose result is awaited many times, because `ValueTask` caches the result for reuse
questions.explanation: The win is on hot paths that frequently complete synchronously (cache hits, buffered reads): `ValueTask` wraps the ready result with zero allocation. When it must complete asynchronously it still wraps a real `Task`, and it must be awaited exactly once â€” not many times.

questions.question: What is the primary performance and compatibility benefit of using a source-generated `JsonSerializerContext` with `System.Text.Json`?
options: It moves type metadata discovery from runtime reflection to compile time, improving startup and making STJ work under Native AOT/trimming
It switches the serializer to operate on UTF-16 strings instead of UTF-8 bytes for faster parsing
It enables JsonPath queries and `JObject`-style dynamic LINQ-to-JSON that STJ otherwise lacks
It automatically caches a singleton `JsonSerializerOptions` so you never rebuild converter caches
questions.explanation: Source generation shifts metadata from runtime reflection to compile time, giving faster startup and being the AOT/trim-safe path since reflection-based serialization is unsupported there. STJ already works on UTF-8 bytes, JsonPath/`JObject` are Newtonsoft features, and caching options is a separate concern.

questions.question: You load 50,000 country codes once at startup and look them up on every request, never mutating the set. Which collection best fits, and why?
options: `FrozenDictionary` â€” high one-time build cost but the fastest read performance, ideal for build-once/read-constantly
`ImmutableDictionary` â€” copy-on-write gives the fastest reads of any immutable collection
`ConcurrentDictionary` â€” required because many requests read the map at the same time
A plain `Dictionary` â€” frozen collections are actually slower to read than a regular `Dictionary`
questions.explanation: `FrozenDictionary` pays a high build cost once and then outperforms `Dictionary`/`HashSet` on lookup, matching the build-once/read-constantly profile. `ImmutableDictionary` is slower to read, a read-only `Dictionary` needs no concurrency control for concurrent reads, and frozen is faster than `Dictionary` on lookups â€” not slower.

questions.question: What does `CollectionsMarshal.GetValueRefOrAddDefault(dict, key, out _)` give you, and what is the main risk?
options: A `ref` to the value slot (adding a default if absent), enabling read-and-update in one hash lookup â€” but it is unsafe and invalidated by a structural change to the dictionary
A defensive copy of the value that you mutate and then write back, doubling the number of hash lookups
An `out` flag indicating whether the key existed, but no way to update the value without a second indexer write
A thread-safe atomic increment of the value, safe to call concurrently from multiple threads
questions.explanation: It returns a `ref` to the value (adding a default if missing), so you read and update in one hash lookup instead of two. The ref is low-level and unsafe: any structural change to the collection can invalidate it, and it offers no thread safety.

questions.question: Does the following method compile, and why?
options: No â€” `Span<byte>` is a `ref struct` that cannot live across an `await`, and `Stream.ReadAsync` takes `Memory<byte>` anyway
Yes â€” the `stackalloc` buffer is promoted into the async state machine on the heap automatically
Yes â€” `Span<byte>` implicitly converts to `Memory<byte>` at the call site, satisfying `ReadAsync`
No â€” `stackalloc` is illegal inside any method marked `async`, even before the first `await`
questions.explanation: A `Span<byte>` is a `ref struct` that cannot be hoisted into the heap-allocated async state machine, so a local span cannot cross an `await`; also `Stream.ReadAsync` accepts `Memory<byte>`, not `Span<byte>`. Use a `Memory<byte>` (e.g. a pooled array), taking `.Span` only in synchronous regions.

questions.question: Constructing a `SearchValues<char>` instance with `SearchValues.Create(...)` inside a hot method on every call is the recommended pattern for SIMD-accelerated `IndexOfAny`.
questions.explanation: `SearchValues<T>` is meant to be built once and cached as a `static readonly` field, since creation precomputes the SIMD-friendly lookup. Rebuilding it per call is exactly what analyzer CA1870 flags.

questions.question: In a portable `Vector<T>`-based loop, why is a scalar tail loop after the vectorized loop necessary?
options: `Vector<int>.Count` (the lane width) is decided at JIT time by the hardware, so the count is rarely an exact multiple â€” the tail handles the leftover elements
The JIT cannot vectorize the final iteration, so the tail re-runs the last `Vector<int>.Count` elements scalarly as a correctness check
`Vector<T>` cannot read the first few elements of a span, so the tail processes the head that the vector loop skipped
Scalar operations are always faster than SIMD on the last elements, so the tail exists purely for speed
questions.explanation: The lane width `Vector<int>.Count` is chosen by the JIT for the CPU (e.g. 8 ints on AVX2), so the input length is usually not an exact multiple; the scalar tail processes the remaining elements the vectorized loop could not. It handles leftover elements, not the head, and is not about speed.

questions.question: Which statement correctly contrasts Native AOT with ReadyToRun (R2R)?
options: Native AOT compiles fully ahead of time with no JIT (fastest startup, lowest memory, but limited reflection); R2R precompiles native code while keeping a JIT fallback and the full feature set
Native AOT keeps the JIT for hot methods while precompiling cold ones, whereas R2R removes the JIT entirely
R2R forbids reflection-based serialization and DI, while Native AOT supports them fully via runtime codegen
Both disable the JIT, but only R2R produces a single self-contained native executable
questions.explanation: Native AOT removes the JIT entirely (fastest startup, lowest memory) at the cost of no runtime codegen and limited reflection, so it needs source generators; R2R is a middle ground that precompiles native code but keeps a JIT fallback and the full feature set. The other options invert these restrictions.
